title: Medical Disorders During Pregnancy
section: Genetics, Aging, and Frontiers in Medicine
authors: Sarah Rae Easter, Robert L. Barbieri

items.subtopic: Medical Disorders During Pregnancy | HYPERTENSION | PREECLAMPSIA
items.title: Diagnosis and Management Thresholds for Preeclampsia
items.content: Preeclampsia is diagnosed by the new onset of hypertension (≥140/90 mmHg) and proteinuria after 20 weeks gestation. Severe preeclampsia includes severe hypertension (≥160/110 mmHg), symptoms like headache or visual changes, pulmonary edema, or lab abnormalities such as thrombocytopenia (<100 × 10⁹/L), renal insufficiency (creatinine >1.1 mg/dL), or liver impairment (transaminases twice normal). A sFlt-1/PlGF ratio ≥40 indicates a high risk of severe preeclampsia within two weeks.
keyPoints: New onset hypertension: ≥140/90 mmHg after 20 weeks gestation
Proteinuria criteria: 24-h urinary protein >300 mg/24 h or protein-creatinine ratio ≥0.3

Severe preeclampsia includes: blood pressure ≥160/110 mmHg, headache not responsive to medications, visual changes, severe epigastric pain, pulmonary edema, thrombocytopenia (<100 × 10⁹/L), renal insufficiency (creatinine >1.1 mg/dL), liver impairment (transaminases twice normal)
sFlt-1/PlGF ratio ≥40 indicates increased risk of severe preeclampsia
Low-dose aspirin initiated between 12 and 14 weeks reduces the risk in high-risk women

items.subtopic: Medical Disorders During Pregnancy | HYPERTENSION | PREECLAMPSIA
items.title: Pathophysiology and Risk Factors of Preeclampsia
items.content: Preeclampsia's pathophysiology involves chronic uteroplacental ischemia, an exaggerated maternal inflammatory response, and imbalance of angiogenic factors. Excess sFlt-1 and decreased PlGF contribute to the syndrome. The sFlt-1/PlGF ratio ≥40 is associated with severe preeclampsia risk. Neurologic manifestations are partly due to cerebral circulatory autoregulation abnormalities, increasing stroke risk at modestly elevated blood pressures.
keyPoints: Pathophysiology involves chronic uteroplacental ischemia and exaggerated maternal inflammatory response
Imbalance of angiogenic factors: excess sFlt-1 and decreased PlGF
sFlt-1/PlGF ratio ≥40 indicates increased risk for severe preeclampsia
Cerebral circulatory autoregulation abnormalities increase stroke risk even at modestly elevated BP
items.reference: Medical Disorders During Pregnancy · Medical Disorders During Pregnancy | HYPERTENSION | PREECLAMPSIA

items.title: Management Strategy for Preeclampsia Without Severe Features
items.content: In cases of preeclampsia without severe features, expectant management is recommended until 37 weeks. This involves close monitoring for the development of severe features or laboratory abnormalities, frequent fetal surveillance, and limiting physical activity to manage blood pressure.
keyPoints: Expectant management until 37 weeks
Close monitoring for severe features or lab abnormalities
Frequent fetal surveillance
Limited physical activity to reduce BP
items.reference: Medical Disorders During Pregnancy · GESTATIONAL HYPERTENSION | Preeclampsia

items.title: Indications and Timing for Delivery in Preeclampsia with Severe Features
items.content: For preeclampsia with severe features, delivery is recommended unless the patient is less than 34 weeks gestation and eligible for expectant management at a tertiary hospital. Indications for earlier delivery include unremitting symptoms, lab abnormalities, or severe hypertension unresponsive to medical treatment.
keyPoints: Delivery recommended unless <34 weeks in a tertiary setting
Indications for early delivery: unremitting symptoms, lab abnormalities, severe BP ≥160/110 mmHg
Use of IV labetalol or hydralazine for managing severe hypertension
items.reference: Medical Disorders During Pregnancy · GESTATIONAL HYPERTENSION | Preeclampsia

items.subtopic: CHRONIC HYPERTENSION | RENAL DISEASE | CARDIAC DISEASE
items.title: Management of Chronic Hypertension in Pregnancy
items.content: Pregnant individuals with chronic hypertension face increased risks for superimposed preeclampsia and placental complications. A prepregnancy evaluation is crucial to identify reversible causes of hypertension and transition off contraindicated medications like ACE inhibitors and ARBs due to their association with stillbirth and congenital anomalies. Labetalol and extended-release nifedipine are preferred for treatment, aiming for a blood pressure target of <140/90 mmHg.
keyPoints: Increased risk of superimposed preeclampsia and placental complications in chronic hypertension
Prepregnancy evaluation to identify remediable causes and transition off ACE inhibitors and ARBs due to increased risk of stillbirth and congenital anomalies
Labetalol and extended-release nifedipine are preferred medications for managing chronic hypertension during pregnancy
Optimal target blood pressure is <140/90 mmHg to reduce risks
items.reference: Medical Disorders During Pregnancy · CHRONIC HYPERTENSION | RENAL DISEASE | CARDIAC DISEASE

items.subtopic: CHRONIC HYPERTENSION | RENAL DISEASE | CARDIAC DISEASE
items.title: Impact of Renal Disease on Pregnancy Outcomes
items.content: Pregnancy can exacerbate hypertension or lead to preeclampsia in patients with underlying renal disease. A prepregnancy serum creatinine level ≥133 μmol/L (≥1.5 mg/dL) and low C4 levels are linked to adverse outcomes, particularly in glomerular diseases. While neither hemodialysis nor prior renal transplant contraindicates pregnancy, both necessitate close management by a multidisciplinary team.
keyPoints: Worsening of hypertension or development of preeclampsia during pregnancy in patients with underlying renal disease
Prepregnancy serum creatinine level ≥133 μmol/L (≥1.5 mg/dL) and low C4 levels associated with adverse outcomes
Close multidisciplinary management required for pregnancies complicated by hemodialysis or prior renal transplant
items.reference: Medical Disorders During Pregnancy · CHRONIC HYPERTENSION | RENAL DISEASE | CARDIAC DISEASE

items.subtopic: VALVULAR HEART DISEASE (See also Chaps. 272–279.) | CONGENITAL HEART DISEASE
items.title: Management of Mitral Stenosis in Pregnancy
items.content: In pregnant women with mitral stenosis, the physiological changes can exacerbate symptoms due to increased blood volume and cardiac output. Those with moderate to severe mitral stenosis (mitral valve area ≤ 1.5 cm²) should undergo valvuloplasty prior to conception if symptomatic or have pulmonary hypertension.
keyPoints: Mitral valve area ≤ 1.5 cm² indicates moderate to severe stenosis.
Pregnancy can increase transmitral pressure gradient, leading to pulmonary edema or tachyarrhythmias.
Valvuloplasty is recommended for symptomatic patients or those with pulmonary hypertension before conception.
Control of heart rate and avoidance of hypovolemia are crucial during labor and delivery.
items.reference: Medical Disorders During Pregnancy · VALVULAR HEART DISEASE (See also Chaps. 272–279.) | CONGENITAL HEART DISEASE

items.subtopic: VALVULAR HEART DISEASE (See also Chaps. 272–279.) | CONGENITAL HEART DISEASE
items.title: Considerations for Aortic Stenosis in Pregnancy
items.content: Pregnancy is generally well-tolerated by women with aortic stenosis if the mean valve gradient is <25 mmHg and left ventricular function is normal. However, symptomatic or severe aortic stenosis (peak gradient >50 mmHg) requires evaluation for correction before pregnancy.
keyPoints: Mean valve gradient <25 mmHg with normal LV function indicates likely tolerance of pregnancy.
Symptomatic or peak gradient >50 mmHg in aortic stenosis necessitates pre-pregnancy evaluation and possible intervention.
items.reference: Medical Disorders During Pregnancy · VALVULAR HEART DISEASE (See also Chaps. 272–279.) | CONGENITAL HEART DISEASE

items.title: Monitoring and Management of Aortic Root Diameter in Pregnancy
items.content: Pregnancy can exacerbate underlying aortopathies, increasing the risk of aortic dissection. Monitoring aortic root diameter is crucial for those at risk, with sequential transthoracic echocardiographic (TTE) monitoring being essential. An aortic root diameter <40 mm suggests a favorable pregnancy outcome, while >50 mm necessitates prepregnancy repair.
keyPoints: Sequential TTE monitoring during pregnancy is vital for assessing the evolution of aortic root diameter in those at risk.
<40 mm aortic root diameter indicates a favorable pregnancy outcome.
>50 mm aortic root diameter requires prepregnancy repair.
Beta blockers are commonly used to manage patients with aortopathy.
items.reference: Medical Disorders During Pregnancy · AORTOPATHY | CARDIAC COMPLICATIONS IN PREGNANCY

items.title: Peripartum Cardiomyopathy: Diagnosis and Management
items.content: Peripartum cardiomyopathy (PPCM) is a serious condition presenting in the third trimester or postpartum with unexplained cardiogenic pulmonary edema. Most patients recover, but those with left ventricular ejection fraction <30% are at high risk for progressive dilated cardiomyopathy. A history of PPCM may indicate genetic predispositions, such as truncating mutations in the titin gene.
keyPoints: PPCM should be considered in unexplained cardiogenic pulmonary edema during or after pregnancy.
Most patients recover completely from PPCM.
<30% left ventricular ejection fraction indicates high risk of progressive dilated cardiomyopathy.
10% with a history of PPCM have truncating mutations in the titin gene, leading to higher rates of ongoing cardiomyopathy.
Left ventricular ejection fraction <50% 12 months postpartum is advised against for subsequent pregnancies.
items.reference: Medical Disorders During Pregnancy · AORTOPATHY | CARDIAC COMPLICATIONS IN PREGNANCY

items.subtopic: ENDOCRINE AND METABOLIC DISORDERS | DIABETES MELLITUS | GESTATIONAL DIABETES
items.title: Understanding Metabolic Changes in Pregnancy and Their Impact on Diabetes Management
items.content: During pregnancy, the fetoplacental unit induces metabolic changes that result in maternal insulin resistance. This is partly due to placental production of steroids, a growth hormone variant, and placental lactogen. These changes can exacerbate hyperglycemia in women with pregestational diabetes mellitus (pgDM), increasing the need for insulin management.
keyPoints: Pregnancy induces maternal insulin resistance due to metabolic changes.
Placental production of steroids, growth hormone variant, and placental lactogen contribute to insulin resistance.
Metabolic changes can exacerbate hyperglycemia in pgDM, increasing insulin needs.
items.reference: Medical Disorders During Pregnancy · ENDOCRINE AND METABOLIC DISORDERS | DIABETES MELLITUS | GESTATIONAL DIABETES

items.subtopic: ENDOCRINE AND METABOLIC DISORDERS | DIABETES MELLITUS | GESTATIONAL DIABETES
items.title: Diagnostic Criteria and Implications of Gestational Diabetes Mellitus (GDM)
items.content: Gestational diabetes mellitus is diagnosed using a two-step strategy at 24–28 weeks gestation. The initial test involves a 50-g oral glucose challenge, with plasma glucose >7.2 mmol/L (>130 mg/dL) prompting further testing. A positive diagnosis of GDM requires elevated glucose values on a subsequent 100-g oral glucose tolerance test (GTT). Treating GDM reduces risks such as preeclampsia and large birth weight.
keyPoints: Screening for GDM is routine at 24–28 weeks gestation.
Initial 50-g glucose challenge: plasma glucose >7.2 mmol/L (>130 mg/dL) prompts further testing.
100-g GTT diagnostic criteria: fasting <5.3 mmol/L (<95 mg/dL), 1 hr <10 mmol/L (<180 mg/dL), 2 hrs <8.6 mmol/L (<155 mg/dL), 3 hrs <7.8 mmol/L (<140 mg/dL).
Two elevated values on GTT confirm GDM diagnosis.
Treatment of GDM reduces risks of preeclampsia and large birth weight.
items.reference: Medical Disorders During Pregnancy · ENDOCRINE AND METABOLIC DISORDERS | DIABETES MELLITUS | GESTATIONAL DIABETES

items.title: Preconception Glycemic Control in pgDM
items.content: Optimizing glycemic control before pregnancy is crucial for individuals with pregestational diabetes mellitus (pgDM) to reduce the risk of fetal congenital malformations. Initiating insulin therapy prior to conception, targeting a hemoglobin A1c level <6%, is recommended.
keyPoints: Preconception counseling and glycemic optimization are cost-effective interventions.
Target preconception hemoglobin A1c <6% to reduce fetal congenital malformations risk.
Insulin is preferred over oral hypoglycemics due to safety and efficacy.
items.reference: Medical Disorders During Pregnancy · Diabetes Mellitus in Pregnancy

items.title: Glycemic Targets During Pregnancy for pgDM
items.content: During pregnancy, intensive glucose management is necessary. Fasting blood glucose should be <5.3 mmol/L (<95 mg/dL), with postprandial targets of <7.8 mmol/L (140 mg/dL) at 1 hour and <6.7 mmol/L (120 mg/dL) at 2 hours. Insulin needs increase throughout pregnancy, requiring adjustments in dosage.
keyPoints: Fasting blood glucose target: <5.3 mmol/L (<95 mg/dL).
Postprandial targets: <7.8 mmol/L (140 mg/dL) at 1 hour and <6.7 mmol/L (120 mg/dL) at 2 hours.
Insulin needs increase from 0.7–0.8 units/kg in the first trimester to 0.9–1.2 units/kg in the third trimester.
items.reference: Medical Disorders During Pregnancy · Diabetes Mellitus in Pregnancy

items.title: Management and Long-term Risks of Gestational Diabetes Mellitus (GDM)
items.content: Treatment for GDM starts with nutritional therapy to achieve normoglycemia, followed by insulin if targets are not met. Metformin is an alternative but has unknown long-term effects on offspring, influencing the preference for insulin. Postpartum, a 2-hour 75-g GTT is recommended within 4-12 weeks to screen for diabetes or impaired glucose tolerance. Patients with GDM have a significantly increased risk of developing type 2 diabetes later in life.
keyPoints: Nutritional therapy is the first line of treatment for GDM.
Insulin is preferred if nutritional therapy fails, while metformin and glyburide are alternatives.
Metformin shows lower mean birth weights and gestational weight gain compared to glyburide and insulin but has unknown long-term effects on children's adiposity.
GDM increases the risk of developing type 2 diabetes by 7- to 10-fold, with a 10% risk within 5 years postpartum.
A 2-hour 75-g GTT is recommended for screening postpartum at 4-12 weeks.
items.reference: Medical Disorders During Pregnancy · Gestational Diabetes | OBESITY

items.title: Impact of Obesity on Pregnancy and Strategies to Mitigate Risks
items.content: Obesity during pregnancy increases the risk of GDM, preeclampsia, cesarean delivery, congenital malformations, stillbirth, and neonatal death. Pre-pregnancy weight reduction can significantly lower these risks. Post-bariatric surgery patients should avoid conception for 12-18 months to stabilize weight. Adhering to gestational weight gain guidelines reduces the risk of macrosomia and cesarean delivery.
keyPoints: Obesity increases risks for GDM, preeclampsia, cesarean delivery, congenital malformations, stillbirth, and neonatal death.
A 10% pre-pregnancy weight reduction can significantly reduce these risks.
Post-bariatric surgery patients should avoid conception for 12-18 months to stabilize weight.
Gestational weight gain guidelines: 25–35 lb (normal weight), 15–25 lb (overweight), and 11–20 lb (obese) to minimize complications.
items.reference: Medical Disorders During Pregnancy · Gestational Diabetes | OBESITY

items.title: Management of Symptomatic Hyperthyroidism in Pregnancy
items.content: In pregnancy, symptomatic hyperthyroidism can be managed with beta blockers, propylthiouracil, or methimazole. Propylthiouracil is preferred in the first trimester due to its lower risk of fetal aplasia cutis compared to methimazole, which crosses the placenta more extensively and carries a higher risk for this condition. Methimazole can be considered after the first trimester if necessary.
keyPoints: Propylthiouracil is preferred in the first trimester due to lower fetal risk.
Methimazole has greater placental transfer and associated risk of fetal aplasia cutis.
Radioiodine should be avoided during pregnancy.
items.reference: Medical Disorders During Pregnancy · THYROID DISEASE | Hyperthyroidism | Hypothyroidism | HEMATOLOGIC DISORDERS

items.title: Therapeutic Approach to Hypothyroidism During Pregnancy
items.content: For hypothyroidism in pregnancy, the goal is to maintain serum TSH within normal ranges using thyroxine. The required dose of thyroxine increases during pregnancy, often necessitating a 30% increase (two additional pills weekly) as early as the fifth week after diagnosis, with subsequent adjustments based on TSH levels.
keyPoints: Thyroxine is used to maintain normal serum TSH levels in hypothyroidism.
Dose of thyroxine typically increases by 30% (two additional pills weekly) from the fifth week of pregnancy.
Adjustments are made according to TSH measurements.
items.reference: Medical Disorders During Pregnancy · THYROID DISEASE | Hyperthyroidism | Hypothyroidism | HEMATOLOGIC DISORDERS

items.subtopic: DEEP VENOUS THROMBOSIS AND PULMONARY EMBOLISM | Venous Thromboembolism
items.title: Management of Anticoagulation in Pregnancy with DVT/PE
items.content: Pregnancy increases the risk of venous thromboembolism (VTE) due to physiological changes that promote a hypercoagulable state. Management involves using low-molecular-weight heparin (LMWH) or unfractionated heparin, with specific timing considerations for neuraxial analgesia during labor.
keyPoints: Pregnancy is associated with increased coagulation factors and decreased protein S and antithrombin III.
DVT or PE occurs in about 1 in 500 pregnancies, highest risk postpartum.
Prophylactic LMWH can be stopped 12 hours before epidural placement; therapeutic LMWH for 24 hours.
Transition to unfractionated heparin near delivery allows shorter waiting time before epidural.
Unfractionated heparin is compatible with an epidural catheter in high-risk patients.
items.reference: Medical Disorders During Pregnancy · DEEP VENOUS THROMBOSIS AND PULMONARY EMBOLISM | Venous Thromboembolism | NEOPLASIA

items.subtopic: DEEP VENOUS THROMBOSIS AND PULMONARY EMBOLISM | NEOPLASIA
items.title: Cancer Management During Pregnancy
items.content: Cancer complicates approximately 1 in 1400 pregnancies, with cervical cancer, breast cancer, melanoma, and lymphoma being the most common. The management of cancer during pregnancy should prioritize maternal physiology, as it generally benefits fetal health too.
keyPoints: Cancer occurs in ~1 in 1400 pregnancies.
Most common cancers: cervical, breast, melanoma, lymphoma.
Pregnancy has little impact on the natural history of malignancies.
Vertical transmission of cancer to fetus is exceedingly rare.
Management should optimize maternal physiology, which also benefits the fetus.
items.reference: Medical Disorders During Pregnancy · DEEP VENOUS THROMBOSIS AND PULMONARY EMBOLISM | Venous Thromboembolism | NEOPLASIA

items.title: Chemotherapy Timing and Fetal Considerations in Pregnancy
items.content: The timing of chemotherapy during pregnancy is critical to minimize fetal risks. Cytotoxic chemotherapy should be avoided in the first trimester due to high risk of spontaneous abortion or malformation. If maternal health necessitates treatment, therapeutic abortion may be considered. Chemotherapy can be administered in the second and third trimesters with lower frequency of toxic effects. Decisions on delaying treatment until post-delivery should consider maternal prognosis and fetal exposure risks.
keyPoints: Cytotoxic chemotherapy is contraindicated in the first trimester due to high risk of spontaneous abortion or malformation.
Chemotherapy can be administered safely in the second and third trimesters with growing evidence supporting neonatal safety.
Decisions on delaying treatment should balance maternal prognosis and fetal exposure risks, considering gestational age at delivery.
items.reference: Medical Disorders During Pregnancy · Malignancy | NEUROLOGIC DISORDERS

items.title: Neurologic Disorders in Pregnancy: Diagnosis and Management
items.content: Neurologic complaints such as headaches or neuropathies are common during pregnancy. Differentiating benign symptoms from life-threatening conditions like cerebrovascular accidents (CVAs) is crucial, especially after 20 weeks of gestation due to the risk of preeclampsia. MRI without gadolinium or noncontrast CT should be used for suspected strokes. Peripheral nerve disorders and restless leg syndrome are also prevalent, with iron supplementation as a treatment if ferritin levels are low.
keyPoints: Neurologic complaints in pregnancy require differentiation from life-threatening conditions like CVAs.
MRI without gadolinium or noncontrast CT is preferred for suspected strokes.
Restless leg syndrome affects up to 20% of pregnant patients, with iron supplementation as a treatment if serum ferritin is low.
items.reference: Medical Disorders During Pregnancy · Malignancy | NEUROLOGIC DISORDERS

items.title: Management of Neurologic Disorders and Epilepsy in Pregnancy
items.content: Pregnancy management for neurologic disorders focuses on medication safety, disease impact, and neonatal outcomes. For epilepsy, lamotrigine and levetiracetam are preferred due to safety data, while valproate is avoided because of congenital malformation risks. Folic acid supplementation at 4 mg daily is recommended for those on antiepileptic drugs (AEDs). Multiple sclerosis patients may experience fewer relapses during pregnancy but increased risk postpartum, with DMTs typically withheld due to limited safety data.
keyPoints: Lamotrigine and levetiracetam are first-line monotherapies for epilepsy in pregnancy due to safety data.
Valproate is contraindicated in pregnancy planning due to congenital malformation risks.
Folic acid supplementation of 4 mg daily is recommended for those taking AEDs.
items.reference: Medical Disorders During Pregnancy · Malignancy | NEUROLOGIC DISORDERS

items.title: Management and Risks of Hyperemesis Gravidarum (HG)
items.content: Hyperemesis gravidarum is a severe form of nausea and vomiting during pregnancy that can lead to dehydration and malnutrition, potentially requiring hospitalization. It may involve the placental hormone GDF15 in its pathogenesis.
keyPoints: Up to 90% of pregnant people experience nausea and vomiting during the first trimester.
HG is severe enough to prevent adequate fluid and nutritional intake.
Consider thiamine, folate supplementation, and electrolyte monitoring for refeeding syndrome.
Evaluate for supplemental enteral nutrition in refractory cases.
items.reference: Medical Disorders During Pregnancy · GASTROINTESTINAL AND LIVER DISEASE

items.title: Diagnosis and Management of Acute Fatty Liver of Pregnancy
items.content: Acute fatty liver of pregnancy is a rare but serious condition characterized by increased serum bilirubin, ammonia levels, and hypoglycemia. It requires prompt diagnosis and management through delivery and supportive care.
keyPoints: Distinguished from HELLP syndrome by markedly increased serum levels of bilirubin and ammonia, and hypoglycemia.
Management includes delivery with supportive care.
Most recover within 7–10 days post-delivery; however, fulminant liver failure can occur.
Association with fetal LCHAD deficiency suggests newborn monitoring and possible genetic testing.
items.reference: Medical Disorders During Pregnancy · GASTROINTESTINAL AND LIVER DISEASE

items.title: Importance of Screening and Treating Asymptomatic Bacteriuria in Pregnancy
items.content: All pregnant patients should be screened for asymptomatic bacteriuria at the first prenatal visit. Treatment is crucial to prevent pyelonephritis, a serious complication during pregnancy.
keyPoints: Screening for asymptomatic bacteriuria with urine culture at the first prenatal visit is recommended.
Treatment of asymptomatic bacteriuforia in pregnant patients is necessary to avoid pyelonephritis.
Progesterone-mediated ureteral and bladder smooth muscle relaxation, along with compression from the enlarging uterus, increases stasis risk.
items.reference: Medical Disorders During Pregnancy · BACTERIAL INFECTIONS

items.title: Management of Pyelonephritis in Pregnancy
items.content: Pregnant women diagnosed with pyelonephritis should receive inpatient IV antibiotic treatment due to the high risk of urosepsis and acute respiratory distress syndrome.
keyPoints: Inpatient IV antibiotic administration is required for pregnant patients with pyelonephritis.
Daily antibiotic suppressive therapy throughout pregnancy is recommended for those with recurrent UTIs or a history of pyelonephritis.
Suppressive treatment options include nitrofurantoin (50 or 100 mg at bedtime) or cephalexin (250 or 500 mg at bedtime).
items.reference: Medical Disorders During Pregnancy · BACTERIAL INFECTIONS

items.title: Screening and Vaccination for Viral Infections in Pregnancy
items.content: Pregnant individuals should undergo screening for hepatitis B, hepatitis C, HIV, and immunity to rubella and varicella. Additionally, vaccination against influenza and COVID-19 is recommended to prevent severe complications.
keyPoints: Screening includes hepatitis B virus, hepatitis C virus, HIV, and immunity to rubella and varicella.
Varicella screening involves history of immunization or infection; antibody testing has low sensitivity.
Influenza vaccine is recommended for all pregnant individuals or those planning pregnancy.
Antiviral treatment should be initiated promptly if influenza is suspected in pregnancy.
All unvaccinated pregnant individuals should receive a COVID-19 vaccine due to increased risk of severe infection.
items.reference: Medical Disorders During Pregnancy · VIRAL INFECTIONS (part 1)

items.title: Management and Risks of Cytomegalovirus and Herpesvirus in Pregnancy
items.content: Primary CMV infection during pregnancy poses a risk for congenital CMV, while genital herpes can lead to severe neonatal outcomes. Management strategies include delaying conception post-CMV infection and administering acyclovir for active genital herpes.
keyPoints: 50–90% of women have antibodies to CMV; primary infection during pregnancy risks congenital CMV.
No effective treatment for CMV in pregnancy has been proven to protect the fetus.
Primary CMV infection should prompt a 6-month delay in conception.
Genital herpes during pregnancy is linked with abortion, prematurity, and neonatal herpes.
Acyclovir (400 mg three times daily) reduces active lesions at term; cesarean delivery for active lesions at labor.
items.reference: Medical Disorders During Pregnancy · VIRAL INFECTIONS (part 1)

items.title: Rubella, Parvovirus, and HIV Infection Management in Pregnancy
items.content: First-trimester rubella poses high teratogenic risks; parvovirus B19 can cause fetal anemia. For HIV, antiretroviral therapy significantly reduces perinatal transmission rates.
keyPoints: Rubella is a known teratogen with highest risk in the first trimester.
Congenital rubella diagnosed by detecting IgM antibodies in fetal blood via umbilical-blood sampling.
Parvovirus B19 can lead to fetal anemia, hydrops, and death; managed by Doppler assessment and possible intrauterine transfusion.
HIV screening for all pregnant women is crucial; ART reduces perinatal transmission from 20% to ~1%.
Viral load guides delivery method: undetectable requires no additional therapy; >1000 copies/mL necessitates IV zidovudine and cesarean.
items.reference: Medical Disorders During Pregnancy · VIRAL INFECTIONS (part 1)

items.title: Zika Virus Management in Pregnancy
items.content: Pregnant individuals with potential Zika virus exposure should undergo specific testing and monitoring. Testing for ZIKV involves serum and urine nucleic acid amplification tests and IgM serology, especially if symptomatic within 2 weeks of exposure. Sequential obstetrical ultrasounds are recommended to monitor fetal growth and detect anomalies.
keyPoints: Zika virus can cause severe microcephaly, CNS malformations, and fetal death.
Testing includes serum and urine nucleic acid amplification tests and IgM serology for symptomatic pregnant individuals with exposure within 2 weeks of symptom onset.
Sequential obstetrical ultrasound is recommended to assess fetal growth and anomalies.
Couples considering pregnancy should avoid travel to areas with known mosquito transmission of ZIKV.
items.reference: Medical Disorders During Pregnancy · VIRAL INFECTIONS (part 2) | VACCINATIONS | MATERNAL MORTALITY

items.title: Understanding Maternal Mortality Rates and Disparities
items.content: Maternal mortality is defined as death related to or aggravated by pregnancy within 42 days of its completion. The U.S. maternal mortality rate has fluctuated, with a notable increase in 2021. Significant racial disparities exist, with non-Hispanic black women experiencing nearly four times the risk compared to non-Hispanic white women.
keyPoints: Maternal death is defined as occurring during pregnancy or within 42 days of its completion from causes related to or aggravated by pregnancy.
The U.S. maternal mortality rate was 32.9/100,000 births in 2021.
Non-Hispanic black women have a nearly fourfold increased risk of maternal death compared to non-Hispanic white women (69.9 vs 26.6 deaths per 100,000 live births).
Women over 40 years old had a maternal mortality rate of 139/100,000 live births.
Cardiovascular disease is the leading cause of pregnancy-related deaths in the U.S.
items.reference: Medical Disorders During Pregnancy · VIRAL INFECTIONS (part 2) | VACCINATIONS | MATERNAL MORTALITY

items.subtopic: SUMMARY | Medical Evaluation of the Patient Undergoing Noncardiac Surgery | EVALUATION OF INTERMEDIATE- AND HIGH-RISK PATIENTS
items.title: Importance of Preconception Counseling in Pregnancy Planning for Patients with Serious Medical Conditions
items.content: Preconception counseling is crucial for patients with serious medical conditions seeking pregnancy. A multidisciplinary team, including internists and maternal-fetal medicine specialists, should counsel these patients to optimize interconception care and manage the risks associated with pregnancy.
keyPoints: More patients with serious medical complications are seeking pregnancy due to advances in diagnostics and infertility treatments.
Improved outcomes require a multidisciplinary team approach for counseling and optimizing interconception care.
Preconception counseling is essential to assess reproductive plans as part of overall health evaluation.
items.reference: Medical Disorders During Pregnancy · SUMMARY | Medical Evaluation of the Patient Undergoing Noncardiac Surgery | EVALUATION OF INTERMEDIATE- AND HIGH-RISK PATIENTS

items.subtopic: SUMMARY | Medical Evaluation of the Patient Undergoing Noncardiac Surgery | EVALUATION OF INTERMEDIATE- AND HIGH-RISK PATIENTS
items.title: Preoperative Risk Assessment for Intermediate and High-Risk Patients Undergoing Noncardiac Surgery
items.content: A thorough preoperative evaluation is necessary for intermediate and high-risk patients undergoing noncardiac surgery. This includes a detailed history, physical examination, 12-lead resting electrocardiogram, and specific laboratory tests based on clinical findings.
keyPoints: Standardized screening questionnaires help identify patients at intermediate or high risk.
Evaluation should start with a thorough history, physical exam, and 12-lead ECG as per ACC/AHA guidelines.
Preoperative lab testing is indicated only for specific conditions identified during clinical examination.
items.reference: Medical Disorders During Pregnancy · SUMMARY | Medical Evaluation of the Patient Undergoing Noncardiac Surgery | EVALUATION OF INTERMEDIATE- AND HIGH-RISK PATIENTS

items.subtopic: PREOPERATIVE CARDIAC RISK ASSESSMENT | TABLE 492-1 Standardized Preoperative Questionnaire $ ^{2} $
items.title: Identifying Low-Risk Procedures for MACE in Pregnancy
items.content: Certain surgeries, such as select ophthalmologic, endoscopic, and superficial procedures, are associated with very low risk (<1%) for major adverse cardiovascular events (MACE) during pregnancy. These patients can proceed to surgery without further cardiac testing.
keyPoints: Low-risk surgeries include select ophthalmologic surgeries (e.g., cataract surgery), endoscopic procedures, and superficial procedures.
Patients undergoing these low-risk procedures should not require additional cardiac testing.
items.reference: Medical Disorders During Pregnancy · PREOPERATIVE CARDIAC RISK ASSESSMENT | TABLE 492-1 Standardized Preoperative Questionnaire $ ^{2} $

items.subtopic: PREOPERATIVE CARDIAC RISK ASSESSMENT | TABLE 492-1 Standardized Preoperative Questionnaire $ ^{2} $
items.title: Using the Revised Cardiac Risk Index (RCRI) for Predicting Perioperative Cardiac Events
items.content: The RCRI uses six clinical predictors to estimate the risk of major cardiac events during surgery. Each predictor is assigned one point, and the presence of three or more predictors indicates a very high risk ($ ≥ $11%) for cardiac complications.
keyPoints: RCRI predictive factors: high-risk surgery, ischemic heart disease, congestive heart failure, cerebrovascular disease, diabetes mellitus treated with insulin, renal insufficiency (creatinine >2.0 mg/dL).
Risk of major cardiac events based on predictors: 0.4% (none), 0.9% (one), 7% (two), 11% (three or more).
items.reference: Medical Disorders During Pregnancy · PREOPERATIVE CARDIAC RISK ASSESSMENT | TABLE 492-1 Standardized Preoperative Questionnaire $ ^{2} $

items.subtopic: PREOPERATIVE CARDIAC RISK ASSESSMENT | TABLE 492-1 Standardized Preoperative Questionnaire $ ^{2} $
items.title: Assessing Functional Capacity in Perioperative Cardiac Risk Evaluation
items.content: Functional capacity is assessed using METs. Patients with moderate or greater functional capacity ($ ≥ $4 METs) generally do not need further noninvasive cardiac testing before elective noncardiac surgery.
keyPoints: Poor exercise tolerance defined as inability to walk four blocks, climb two flights of stairs, or meet a MET level of 4.
Moderate or greater functional capacity ($ ≥ $4 METs) typically does not require additional cardiac testing.
items.reference: Medical Disorders During Pregnancy · PREOPERATIVE CARDIAC RISK ASSESSMENT | TABLE 492-1 Standardized Preoperative Questionnaire $ ^{2} $

items.subtopic: ☑ PREOPERATIVE NONINVASIVE CARDIAC TESTING FOR RISK STRATIFICATION
items.title: When to Consider Preoperative Noninvasive Cardiac Testing
items.content: Preoperative noninvasive cardiac testing should be reserved for patients with poor or unknown cardiac capacity if the results would alter clinical management or modify perioperative care. Routine application is not supported by evidence.
keyPoints: Testing is indicated in patients with poor or unknown cardiac capacity.
The goal of testing is to alter clinical management or modify perioperative care.
Limited role for CCTA in noncardiac surgery unless it would change patient management.

items.title: Role of Coronary Revascularization Before Noncardiac Surgery
items.content: Coronary revascularization is not recommended solely to reduce perioperative cardiac events. It should be considered if indicated by clinical guidelines, irrespective of planned noncardiac surgery.
keyPoints: Revascularization before noncardiac surgery does not reduce perioperative cardiac events.
Consider revascularization based on clinical practice guidelines, not just for surgery.
Coronary Artery Revascularization Prophylaxis trial showed no difference in outcomes with or without preoperative revascularization; left main disease patients were excluded.

items.subtopic: RISK MODIFICATION: PREVENTIVE STRATEGIES TO REDUCE CARDIAC RISK (part 1)
items.title: When to Delay Noncardiac Surgery After PCI
items.content: Elective noncardiac surgery should be delayed for 6 months after elective PCI and 12 months after acute coronary syndrome. However, if time-sensitive noncardiac surgery is necessary, it can be considered as soon as 1 month after starting DAPT.
keyPoints: Delay elective noncardiac surgery for 6 months post-PCI and 12 months post-acute coronary syndrome.
Consider time-sensitive noncardiac surgery at least 1 month after initiating DAPT.
items.reference: Medical Disorders During Pregnancy · RISK MODIFICATION: PREVENTIVE STRATEGIES TO REDUCE CARDIAC RISK (part 1)

items.subtopic: RISK MODIFICATION: PREVENTIVE STRATEGIES TO REDUCE CARDIAC RISK (part 1)
items.title: Perioperative Use of Beta-Blockers
items.content: Beta-blocker therapy should be continued in patients already on it, and not started or withdrawn on the day of surgery. Initiation may be considered perioperatively for high-risk patients with intermediate- or high-risk ischemia or three or more RCRI risk factors.
keyPoints: Continue beta-blockers in patients already taking them pre-surgery.
Avoid starting or stopping beta-blockers on the day of surgery.
Consider initiation for high-risk patients with significant ischemic risk or multiple RCRI factors.
items.reference: Medical Disorders During Pregnancy · RISK MODIFICATION: PREVENTIVE STRATEGIES TO REDUCE CARDIAC RISK (part 1)

items.subtopic: RISK MODIFICATION: PREVENTIVE STRATEGIES TO REDUCE CARDIAC RISK (part 1)
items.title: Perioperative Use of Statins
items.content: Continue statin therapy in patients already on it to reduce perioperative cardiac risk. Initiation is reasonable for vascular surgery but not conclusively beneficial for other high-risk procedures.
keyPoints: Continue statins in patients undergoing noncardiac surgery with established atherosclerosis.
Initiate statins for vascular surgery regardless of clinical risk.
No clear benefit from initiating statins perioperatively for other elevated-risk surgeries.
items.reference: Medical Disorders During Pregnancy · RISK MODIFICATION: PREVENTIVE STRATEGIES TO REDUCE CARDIAC RISK (part 1)

items.subtopic: RISK MODIFICATION: PREVENTIVE STRATEGIES TO REDUCE CARDIAC RISK (part 2)
items.title: Management of DAPT in Perioperative Period
items.content: Decisions regarding dual antiplatelet therapy (DAPT) around noncardiac surgery are crucial to minimize stent thrombosis risk while balancing bleeding concerns. The duration and interruption strategies for DAPT depend on the type of stent implanted and the urgency of surgery.
keyPoints: Bare metal stents: DAPT can be interrupted 30 days post-implantation for noncardiac surgery.
Drug-eluting stents (DES): Continue DAPT for at least 6 months; interrupt 6 months post-implantation if necessary.
Acute coronary syndrome with DES: DAPT should last at least 12 months.
Elective surgery timing after P2Y12 inhibitors: Clopidogrel - 5 days, Prasugrel - 7 days, Ticagrelor - 3–5 days.
Aspirin is continued perioperatively unless bleeding risk is catastrophic.
items.reference: Medical Disorders During Pregnancy · RISK MODIFICATION: PREVENTIVE STRATEGIES TO REDUCE CARDIAC RISK (part 2)

items.subtopic: RISK MODIFICATION: PREVENTIVE STRATEGIES TO REDUCE CARDIAC RISK (part 2)
items.title: Avoidance of Alpha-2 Agonists in Noncardiac Surgery
items.content: Alpha-2 agonists, such as clonidine, are not recommended for preventing cardiac events during noncardiac surgery due to increased risk of adverse effects.
keyPoints: POISE-2 trial findings: Clonidine increases nonfatal cardiac arrest and hypotension rates.
Reduces the rate of death or nonfatal myocardial infarction (MI).
Not recommended for perioperative cardiac event prevention.
items.reference: Medical Disorders During Pregnancy · RISK MODIFICATION: PREVENTIVE STRATEGIES TO REDUCE CARDIAC RISK (part 2)

items.subtopic: RISK MODIFICATION: PREVENTIVE STRATEGIES TO REDUCE CARDIAC RISK (part 2)
items.title: SGLT-2 Inhibitors and Perioperative Management
items.content: Sodium-glucose cotransporter 2 inhibitors should be paused before noncardiac surgery due to the risk of euglycemic diabetic ketoacidosis, particularly in the context of perioperative fasting.
keyPoints: SGLT-2 inhibitors are associated with beneficial effects for heart failure and diabetes management.
Risk of euglycemic diabetic ketoacidosis, a rare but serious complication.
Therapy should be interrupted 3–4 days prior to scheduled noncardiac surgery.
items.reference: Medical Disorders During Pregnancy · RISK MODIFICATION: PREVENTIVE STRATEGIES TO REDUCE CARDIAC RISK (part 2)

items.title: Identifying High-Risk Patients for Postoperative Pulmonary Complications
items.content: Patients undergoing specific types of surgeries or under certain conditions are at elevated risk for postoperative pulmonary complications. These include emergency surgery, prolonged procedures, and major surgeries such as aortic aneurysm repair, vascular surgery, and surgeries involving the abdominal, thoracic, neurologic, head, or neck regions.
keyPoints: Patients undergoing emergency or prolonged (3–4 h) surgery; aortic aneurysm repair; vascular surgery; major abdominal, thoracic, neurologic, head, or neck surgery; and general anesthesia are at elevated risk for postoperative pulmonary complications.
items.reference: Medical Disorders During Pregnancy · PREOPERATIVE PULMONARY RISK ASSESSMENT

items.title: Utilizing the ARISCAT Risk Index for Postoperative Pulmonary Complications
items.content: The ARISCAT risk index is a tool used to estimate postoperative pulmonary complications by evaluating seven specific risk factors. These include age, low preoperative oxygen saturation, recent respiratory infection, type of surgery, duration of surgery, hemoglobin levels, and emergency status.
keyPoints: ARISCAT risk index accounts for: age, low preoperative oxygen saturation, respiratory infection within the preceding month, upper abdominal or thoracic surgery, surgery lasting >2 h, hemoglobin <10 g/dL, and emergency surgery.
items.reference: Medical Disorders During Pregnancy · PREOPERATIVE PULMONARY RISK ASSESSMENT

items.title: Perioperative Management of Diabetes Mellitus: Target Glucose and Insulin Use
items.content: In the perioperative period, intensive glycemic control is not recommended for diabetic patients due to increased risk of hypoglycemia. Instead, a target glucose range of 100-180 mg/dL should be maintained. Oral hypoglycemic agents are contraindicated on the morning of surgery; instead, insulin therapy (IV infusion or subcutaneous sliding-scale) is advised for managing perioperative hyperglycemia.
keyPoints: Target glucose range in perioperative period: 100-180 mg/dL
Intensive glycemic control not recommended due to hypoglycemia risk
Oral hypoglycemics contraindicated on surgery morning
Use IV infusion of short-acting insulin or subcutaneous sliding-scale insulin for hyperglycemia
items.reference: Medical Disorders During Pregnancy · DIABETES MELLITUS | Preoperatively | Intraoperatively | Postoperatively | INFECTIVE ENDOCARDITIS | AORTIC STENOSIS

items.title: Prophylaxis and Surgical Considerations in Patients with Infective Endocarditis Risk and Aortic Stenosis
items.content: For patients at risk of infective endocarditis, prophylactic antibiotics are necessary before dental procedures involving gingival tissue manipulation or oral mucosa perforation. In aortic stenosis, while severe cases previously posed high surgical risks, recent data suggests that selected patients can undergo noncardiac surgery with acceptable outcomes. Severe symptomatic aortic stenosis warrants valve intervention if possible, whereas asymptomatic patients with preserved ejection fraction may safely proceed with low- to intermediate-risk surgeries.
keyPoints: Prophylactic antibiotics required for high-risk infective endocarditis patients before certain dental procedures
Severe symptomatic aortic stenosis: consider valve intervention if surgery can be deferred
Asymptomatic severe aortic stenosis with preserved ejection fraction: low- to intermediate-risk surgeries generally safe
30-day mortality in severe aortic stenosis patients undergoing moderate/high-risk surgery: 5.9% vs matched controls at 3.1%, p = .13
items.reference: Medical Disorders During Pregnancy · DIABETES MELLITUS | Preoperatively | Intraoperatively | Postoperatively | INFECTIVE ENDOCARDITIS | AORTIC STENOSIS

items.title: Prophylaxis Strategies for Venous Thromboembolism During Pregnancy
items.content: Perioperative prophylaxis of venous thromboembolism should adhere to guidelines from the American College of Chest Physicians. Aspirin alone is ineffective as a single agent for thrombophylaxis. For moderate-risk patients, options include low-dose unfractionated heparin (≤5000 units SC bid), low-molecular-weight heparin like enoxaparin (30 mg bid or 40 mg qd), or fondaparinux (2.5 mg qd). High-risk patients should receive unfractionated heparin at 5000 units SC tid. Direct oral anticoagulants, such as rivaroxaban, may serve as alternatives to traditional prophylactic doses and have shown efficacy in reducing venous thromboembolic events post nonmajor orthopedic surgery without increasing bleeding risk significantly.
keyPoints: Follow American College of Chest Physicians guidelines for perioperative VTE prophylaxis
Aspirin alone is not effective for thrombophylaxis
Moderate-risk patients: low-dose unfractionated heparin (≤5000 units SC bid), enoxaparin (30 mg bid or 40 mg qd), fondaparinux (2.5 mg qd)
High-risk patients: unfractionated heparin (5000 units SC tid)
Direct oral anticoagulants like rivaroxaban can be alternatives, reducing VTE events without significant bleeding increase
Graduated compression stockings and pneumatic compression devices are useful supplements or alternatives for those at high bleeding risk
items.reference: Medical Disorders During Pregnancy · VENOUS THROMBOEMBOLISM

items.title: Alternative Prophylaxis Options in High Bleeding Risk Patients
items.content: In patients with a significant risk of bleeding, graduated compression stockings and pneumatic compression devices can be used as supplements to anticoagulant therapy or as alternatives. These methods are particularly useful when the use of anticoagulants poses an excessive bleeding risk.
keyPoints: Use graduated compression stockings and pneumatic compression devices for patients at high bleeding risk
These methods serve as supplements or alternatives to anticoagulant therapy
